source: Numerics/Data/Statistics/Histogram.cs; the 69-value sample1, Rice-rule 10-bin lower/upper/frequency, and explicit 20-bin lower/upper/frequency arrays are scraped verbatim from Test_Numerics/Data/Statistics/Test_Histogram.cs (Test_Histogram_Basic/Test_Histogram_Specified/Test_Stats/Test_DataCount/Test_Indexing).
reference: Numerics @ a2c4dbf. Args convention (this port's own -- see fixtures/README.md): args = [explicit_bins, data...] for the whole-histogram scalar targets (explicit_bins == 0 selects the Rice-Rule ctor `new Histogram(data)`; explicit_bins > 0 selects `new Histogram(data, explicit_bins)`); the bin_lower_bound_at/bin_upper_bound_at/bin_frequency_at/get_bin_index_of element-lookup targets append one trailing probe value (a 0-based bin index, or an x value to look up) after `data`. mean/median/mode/standard_deviation values were independently recomputed in Python from Test_Stats.cs's own closed-form formulas applied to the scraped lower/upper/frequency arrays (matching that test's 1e-6 tolerance); bin edge/frequency element values are the scraped Excel-derived literals (1e-3 tolerance, matching that test); get_bin_index_of cases port Test_Indexing's invariant (GetBinIndexOf(bin[i].Midpoint) == i) with the midpoint independently recomputed in Python from the identical bin-construction arithmetic. Every non-integer-literal value here was additionally confirmed reproducible against the REAL C# Histogram by tools/verify_oracles.py.
